coredata.dc:title: Rapid fluorescence detection of black tea fermentation degree based on cobalt ion mediated carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Shuci
Dong, Shuai
Chen, Yurong
Shen, Jingfei
Cui, Qingqing
Sun, Changying
Ning, Jingming
Li, Luqing
coredata.dc:description: Fermentation is key to the quality of black tea, and traditional sensory evaluation methods are too slow and subjective for reliable, large-scale evaluation during fermentation. To address this problem, this study formulated a sensing system based on cobalt ions-doped carbon quantum dots emitting bright blue fluorescence. In evaluation experiments, this system was found to work stably for 90 min and accurately determine fermentation quality in real time. In fluorescence spectra results, fluorescence intensity decreased sharply with the concentrations of catechins, theaflavins, and gallic acid and this relationship was highly linear at concentrations from 0.01 to 50 μg/mL; R2 was >97% and the limit of detection was <0.046 μg/mL. A least-squares support vector machine model was established, and it had a 100% accuracy rate in discriminating between degrees of fermentation. Therefore, this quantum-dot-based system serves as an accurate and efficient means of measuring the fermentation quality of black tea.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fermentation quality
Catechins
Theaflavins
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence spectra

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of excitation wavelength and reaction time
Quenching of CQDs’ fluorescence by representative components of black tea and development of standard curves
Qualitative differentiation of black tea samples with different degrees of fermentation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of the CQDs. (B) Size distribution of the CQDs, with an average diameter of 3.30 nm. (C) XPS spectrum of the CQDs. (D) The FTIR spectra of the CQDs. (E) CIE color coordinates of the CQDs, the color coordinates are (0.148, 0.104). (F) The UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the CQDs. (G) Time stability of CQD.

The effect of the cobalt ion: analyte volume ratio on the system's detection performance. (A) 0.05 mL of EGCG, (B) 0.1 mL of EGCG and (C) 0.2 mL of EGCG were added to a system containing 0.1 mL of cobalt nitrate solution.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CQD under the different excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 400 nm. (B) Reaction time during sample testing, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the first test.

Changes in the fluorescence intensity of Co-CQDs sensor after adding different concentrations of catechins or theaflavins. The corresponding fitting curve and the limit of detection is shown in the illustration. (A) C, (B) EC, (C) GC (D) EGC, (E) ECG, (F) GCG, (G) EGCG, (H) GC, (I) TF, (J) TF-3-G, (K) TF-3′-G and (L) TF-D-G.
Qualitative results of black tea samples with different degrees of fermentation (A) Principal component analysis score plot (B) Hierarchical cluster analysis plot.
Prediction models for black tea with different fermentation levels.

head.ce:title: Rapid fluorescence detection of black tea fermentation degree based on cobalt ion mediated carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization, Key Laboratory of Tea Biology and Tea Processing of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, International Joint Research Laboratory of Tea Chemistry and Health Effects of Ministry of Education, Anhui Provincial Laboratory, Hefei, 230036, Anhui, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization
Key Laboratory of Tea Biology and Tea Processing of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs
International Joint Research Laboratory of Tea Chemistry and Health Effects of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization, Key Laboratory of Tea Biology and Tea Processing of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, International Joint Research Laboratory of Tea Chemistry and Health Effects of Ministry of Education, Anhui Provincial Laboratory, Hefei, 230036, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fermentation is key to the quality of black tea, and traditional sensory evaluation methods are too slow and subjective for reliable, large-scale evaluation during fermentation. To address this problem, this study formulated a sensing system based on cobalt ions-doped carbon quantum dots emitting bright blue fluorescence. In evaluation experiments, this system was found to work stably for 90 min and accurately determine fermentation quality in real time. In fluorescence spectra results, fluorescence intensity decreased sharply with the concentrations of catechins, theaflavins, and gallic acid and this relationship was highly linear at concentrations from 0.01 to 50 μg/mL; R2 was >97% and the limit of detection was <0.046 μg/mL. A least-squares support vector machine model was established, and it had a 100% accuracy rate in discriminating between degrees of fermentation. Therefore, this quantum-dot-based system serves as an accurate and efficient means of measuring the fermentation quality of black tea.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Black tea is one of the main types of tea produced in and exported by China. It can be divided into three categories on the basis of the technique with which it is processed: Congou, Souchong, and CTC (short for crush, tear, curl) black tea. Congou black tea is China's most widely distributed and widely produced black tea variety. The processing of Keemun black tea, a type of Congou black tea, involves withering, rolling, fermentation, and drying. Fermentation is crucial to the quality of black tea (Wu et al., 2019) because of its effects of colour, taste, and flavor. (Chen et al., 2023). Along with the fermentation of black tea, the content of catechins decreases, while its oxidation product theaflavins show a tendency to increase and then decrease. Catechins account for the largest proportion of polyphenols in black tea and catechin monomers have a polyhydroxy-like structure and considerable antioxidant activity (Stodt et al., 2014). Changes in polyphenols are an important basis for evaluating the degree of fermentation.

The monitoring and control of quality have been a hot research topic in the field of tea processing. The traditional methods for evaluating the fermentation quality of black tea include sensory review method and chemical analysis method. In sensory review, the fermentation quality of black tea is determined by experienced experts, but this method lacks quantitative basis and is prone to subjective bias. The chemical analysis method is based on the content of chemical components such as catechins and TFs, and the quality of black tea fermentation is judged according to the changes in the content of these chemical components (Chen et al., 2023), For example, the electronic nose technique, which determines the degree of black tea fermentation based on changes in the content of tea flavouring substances during fermentation, is susceptible to environmental and equipment-specific factors (e.g. detector drift) (Tozlu & Okumus, 2018). In electronic tongue technology, a variety of functional bionic detection sensors are employed to obtain a total characteristic signal of a sample, and the characteristic information of the liquid sample is identified using an appropriate pattern recognition method to determine the degree of fermentation; however, the sensors employed in such systems have limitations, and a single electronic tongue cannot be used to detect all substances (Dang et al., 2019). Computer vision system collects more diverse and faster information than traditional inspection methods. Computer vision system are based on the appearance of tea leaves, such as their morphology and colour, and this information is combined with chemometric data; however, machine vision cannot be used to analyse the chemical components underlying fermentation (Ren, Gan, Song, Ning, & Zhang, 2021). However, computer vision system are susceptible to environmental influences when acquiring image information and cannot be used to fully analyse the underlying chemical composition of fermentation. Thus, new methods such as those based on chemical analysis and spectroscopic techniques, must be developed.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon-based material composed of carbon polymers with particle size generally between 1 and 10 nm, which suffer from the disadvantages of low fluorescence yield and fluorescence intensity susceptibility to environmental pH (Budimir et al., 2021). CQDs have many hydrophilic functional groups on their surface, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups (Tian et al., 2020). The fluorescence intensity of CQDs in aqueous solution can be kept stable for a long period (Bayati et al, 2018, Dai, Zambrana, Rees, & Fidalgo de Cortalezzi, 2018). In addition, CQDs have the advantages of easy preparation, low price, non-toxicity, large specific surface area, superconductivity, high electron transfer capability and electron storage capability. They have thus been successfully applied in sensors, imaging technology, luminescent materials, and photoelectric catalysts for food analysis and detection (Shen et al., 2019). The hydrothermal method is commonly used to prepare CQDs and has the advantages of simple operation, easy control, and environmental sustainability among other method (Schneider, Bartsch, Stark, & Grass, 2019). Because of their unique fluorescence and optical properties and stability, CQDs are widely employed in food analysis and other fields. For example, in the analysis of foodborne pathogens, carbon quantum dots and aptamers were combined as a new fluorescent probe for the sensitive quantitative detection of Salmonella typhi (Wang et al, 2015, Xu, Zhang, & Jiang, 2015). In the detection of food additives, eucalyptus was used as a natural carbon source, and carbon quantum dots were prepared as a fluorescent probe for the detection of lemon yellow, a food additive (Ghereghlou, Esmaeili, & Darroudi, 2021). In the field of food microbial toxin detection, a N-CQD/aptamer/AuNPs nanocomposite was developed for fluorescence detection of AFB1 in peanuts and maize (Wang et al., 2016). As well as in pesticide residue detection, carbon quantum dots were synthesised using rosemary leaves as a carbon source, and the synthesised carbon dots were modified with molecularly imprinted polymers, which were then used as fluorophores in optical sensors for the detection of thiabendazole residues in fruit juices (Kazemifard, Ensafi, & Rezaei, 2020). For heavy metal detection, blue fluorescent Si/CDs were successfully synthesised by conventional hydrothermal method, which can be used as sensitive fluorescent sensors to detect Hg2+ ions in water (Guo et al., 2022). However, the feasibility of using CQDs for monitoring of black tea fermentation quality has not yet been explored.

CQDs emit fluorescence when they are excited by light of specific wavelengths, and the addition of specific substances leads to the fluorescence being quenched, restored, enhanced, or of a different wavelength. CQDs thus have great potential as a fluorescent probe. Metal ions, which are strong oxidants, facilitate the ultrasensitive detection of trace organic molecules by CQDs. Lü et al. (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020) found that the fluorescence of CQDs could be effectively quenched by Fe3+ due to the chelation between Fe3+ and functional groups on the surface of the CQDs; by introducing l-ascorbic acid (AA), the fluorescence of the CQDs could be mostly recovered because the Fe3+ was reduced by the AA. The CQDs sensing system could thus be used for the detection of AA. The reaction of metal ions with the substances within tea leaves is a topic worthy of exploration. The linearity of the degree of change in fluorescence over a range of concentrations is the main basis for quantitative detection by CQDs (Molaei, 2019).

The aim of this study was to develop a new rapid assay for detecting the degree of fermentation of black tea. The study can be summarized as follows: In this study, catechins, TFs and gallic acid, the amounts of which change considerably during the fermentation of black tea, were employed as analytes to develop an effective method of fermentation quality monitoring, with this method involving the fluorescence property of CQDs. First, CQDs were prepared and characterised. Second, the components of the sensing system were optimised, and the response time of the sensing system to the sample was determined. A Co-CQDs detection system was then constructed for the quantitative detection of key substances in black tea during fermentation. Finally, we developed and compared the accuracy of three qualitative identification models—extreme learning machine (ELM), support vector machine (SVM), and least-squares support vector machine (LSSVM) models—to determine the degree of fermentation of black tea. This study provides a series of innovative methods for real-time, reliable assessment of black tea fermentation quality, addressing the problem that traditional sensory assessment methods are too slow and subjective during large-scale assessment.

The black tea samples of various fermentation degrees used in this study came from the same batch of samples from Xiangyuan Tea Factory, Jinzhai County, Lu'an City, Anhui Province, China, in April 2021. The fresh tea leaves collected were local population varieties, and mainly one bud and two leaves. The tea leaves were kneaded and placed in a fermentation chamber at a temperature of 25 °C and air humidity of 95% for a total of 6 h. During the fermentation process, the tea leaves contained approximately 60% water. At the beginning of fermentation, the leaves were generally greenish and emitted an unpleasant grassy smell. As the fermentation progressed, the leaf colour changed from lime green to reddish yellow to red, and the grassy flavor gradually changed to a pleasant floral and fruity flavor. Excessive fermentation causes leaves to turn dark red, the sweetness to diminish, and a slight sourness to appear (Muthumani & Kumar, 2007). In the present study, black tea processing was performed in accordance with the Technical Regulations for Black Tea Processing (GB/T 35810-2018). From the beginning of fermentation, samples were collected at 1-h intervals by using a five-point sampling method; 20 samples were obtained in parallel and mixed well each time to ensure the representativeness of the samples. All samples were sequentially packed into numbered sealed bags and subsequently stored at −80 °C.

A team of seven professional reviewers was formed to perform sensory evaluations of black tea samples with different degrees of fermentation The tea samples used for sensory evaluation were all fermented samples but were freeze-dried beforehand to avoid the influence of other factors. The reviewers referred to the factors for evaluating black tea in the (GB/T 23776-2018) regulations. The weights of the infused leaf, infusion colour, flavor, and taste factors were set at 15%, 25%, 30%, and 30%, respectively. Because the same batch of raw materials was used throughout the study, shape would not have offered discriminatory power and was thus not included in the evaluation. Insufficient fermentation or excessive fermentation was indicated by 70–84 points, and moderate fermentation was indicated by 85–99 points. Through the sensory review, the reviewers categorised the black tea samples fermented for 0–2, 3–5, and 6 h as being insufficiently fermented, moderately fermented, and excessively fermented, respectively (Table S1). And the relevant physicochemical composition information is shown in Table S2.

This study improved upon the existing method of synthesising CQDs (Zhu et al., 2019). CQDs solution was successfully prepared using the hydrothermal synthesis method, with sodium citrate employed as the carbon source. Regarding the specific experimental operation, 0.28 mmol sodium citrate was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water; 1.26 mmol urea and 0.42 mmol of thiourea were added and mixed in; and the solution was then processed for 10 min by using an ultrasonic processor. Once the reagent had completely dissolved, it was transferred to an autoclave chamber lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to obtain purified brownish-yellow CQDs solution, which was stored at 4 °C away from light. The chemicals used were of analytical purity.

The morphology and size of the CQDs were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT7700), and the dots' elemental composition was investigated using X-ray energy spectrometry (X-Max N 150). A 100:1 mixture of potassium bromide and CQDs solid was ground, and the surface functional groups of the CQDs were then characterised through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolette is 50). In addition, solutions with certain CQDs concentrations were prepared, and their absorption spectra were obtained using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer with a detection range of 200–600 nm. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Aglient Technologies) was employed to obtain excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs and determine the dots' optimal excitation wavelength. To investigate the stability of the CQDs in aqueous solution, the operation was performed three times at 5-min intervals, and the duration of the dots’ stability in aqueous solution was determined by calculating the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the fluorescence value emitted at 440 nm for a given time.

The mixing of CQDs with metal ions is an effective means of optimising the dots’ sensing performance (Khataee, Jalili, Dastborhan, Karimi, & Azar, 2020). The effects of various metal ions on the detection performance of the CQDs sensing system was thus determined. First, 0.1 mL of 10−3 mol/L Fe(NO3)·9H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, ZnCl2, Co(NO3)2·6H2O or Cu(NO3)2 solution was added to solution containing 2.7 mL of water, 0.1 mL of CQDs, and 0.1 mL of EGCG. The solution was then centrifuged after being well shaken, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and a detection band of 350–600 nm. The effect of the different metal ions on the detection of EGCG was determined by calculating the coefficient of the correlation between the EGCG concentration and the amount of fluorescence emitted by the CQDs at 440 nm.

Subsequently, the quantitative relationship between the metal ions and analyte was analysed. The total volume of the fixed reaction system was 3 mL; the system was formed using 0.1 mL of CQDs solution, 0.1 mL of 10−3 mol metal ion solution, and EGCG at a volume of 0.05, 0.1, or 0.2 mL, with the remainder being pure water. The effect of the metal ion:analyte volume ratio on the system's detection performance was assessed by comparing the coefficients of correlation between the EGCG concentration and intensity of 440-nm fluorescence emitted by the CQDs under the same detection conditions.

Finally, the time it took for the CQDs to respond to the target was investigated on the basis of the optimal conditions obtained from the aforementioned experiments. EGCG (10 μg/mL) was added to the optimal detection system. The total detection time was 8 min, with the response assessed in 30-s intervals, and the operation was performed three times with three sets of spectral data obtained for each time point; thus, 48 sets of spectral data were collected in total. The fluorescence intensity at each time point is divided by the fluorescence intensity at the first time point to determine the reaction time.

Before extraction could be performed using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), Catechin (C, 99%), epicatechin (EC, 98%), gallocatechin (GC, 99%), epigallocatechin (EGC, 98%), epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG, 99%), epicatechin gallate (ECG, 98%), gallocatechin gallate (GCG, 99%), gallic acid (GA, 99%), theaflavin (TF, 98%), theaflavin-3-gallate (TF-3-G, 98%), theaflavin-3′-gallate (TF-3′-G, 98%), and theaflavin-3,3′-digallate (TF-D-G, 99%) were purchased from Maclean Co., Itd.

Standard solutions were prepared in the following concentrations: 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL. To prevent or slow the oxidative decomposition of the standards, they were prepared to the required concentration by using anhydrous ethanol and stored in a sealed container and protected from light in a refrigerator at 4°C until their use.

The black tea samples were extracted as follows: Firstly, 3 g of black tea fermentation sample was weighed and steeped in 150 mL 100°C water for 5 min. Then, the tea infusion was filtered and quickly cooled to room temperature.

A Carry-Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies was used to obtain fluorescence information; the settings were an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and a detection band of 350–600 nm. To obtain an accurate fluorescence profile of a sample, the spectroscopic instrument was calibrated and zeroed prior to each test. Before spectral information was collected, we added 2.7 mL of ultrapure water to a 5 mL centrifuge tube and then added 0.1 mL of CQDs solution, 0.1 mL of 10−3 mol/L cobalt ion solution, and 0.1 mL of standard substance dissolved in the anhydrous ethanol. The tube was centrifuged at 5620 rpm for 5 min. The fluorescence profile of the tube's upper solution was then measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Five parallel measurements were made for each sample with three spectra recorded each time for a total of nine spectra per sample; 165 spectra were retained in total. All optical measurements were conducted under natural conditions at room temperature. For tea samples, the same procedure was followed; 2.7 mL of ultrapure water, 0.1 mL of CQDs solution, 0.1 mL of 10−3 mol/L cobalt ion solution, and 0.1 mL of tea infusion were added to a 5 mL centrifuge tube. After centrifugation, the upper layer of liquid was get, and its fluorescence spectrum was obtained. The measurement was performed six times in parallel for each sample, and five spectra were recorded each time, meaning a total of 30 spectra for each sample; 210 spectra in total were used in the statistical analysis.

The fluorescence spectral data collected were smoothed using only the Unscrambler X 10.4 (CAMO, Norway) software. Standard curves for 12 key substances were established and the relationship between fluorescence intensity and sample concentration was analysed using the Stern-Volmer equation (Cao et al., 2022) with the following equation:
F 0
F
− 1 =
K
s v
[ c ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of the sample to the CQDs solution, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [c] is the concentration of the sample. The limit of detection (LOD) for each standard was calculated using the following equation (Cao et al., 2022):
L O D =
3 N
S
where N is the standard deviation of the blank sample and S is the slope.

Principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) were then used to investigate the effective grouping of fermentation levels by the quenching of the CQDs’ fluorescence in black tea samples when fermentation was performed for different durations. PCA and HCA were all performed in Origin 2023 (OriginLab Corp. Massachusetts, USA) software. For analysis of the PCA results, we employed two principal components to represent the sample information. PCA is a commonly used unsupervised machine-learning technique that reduces the dimensionality of a dataset by identifying the most important principal components of the data that explain differences in the data. PCA can be used to transfer originally high-dimensional data into a low-dimensional space, thereby capturing the most important patterns and relationships between variables (Siger & Gornas, 2023). HCA is another commonly used unsupervised machine-learning algorithm, the aim of which is to obtain the structure of clusters in samples while discovering the hierarchy of the truncated version of the distribution by grouping similar observations together. The main difference between HCA and PCA is their objectives: the objective of PCA is to reduce the dimensionality of the dataset, whereas that of HCA is to group similar samples together. In addition, the output of PCA is a new coordinate system and corresponding principal components, whereas the output of HCA is a tree diagram in which each leaf node represents a sample and the internal nodes indicate the clustering relationships between the samples (Siger & Gornas, 2023).

Finally, qualitative models were constructed using supervised machine-learning techniques, without pre-processing of imported data. ELM, SVM, and LSSVM models were constructed in MATLAB 2014a (Mathworks, Natick, USA). Before these models were constructed, we divided the samples into calibration and prediction sets in a 2:1 ratio, setting the spectral data as input variables and the degree of fermentation as the output variable. The ELM is a feed-forward neural-network-based machine-learning system for solving both supervised and unsupervised learning problems. The ELM algorithm allows for the random initialisation of the input weights and biases to obtain the corresponding output weights. The model is constructed by comparing the effect of various numbers of neurons on the classification accuracy and selecting the optimal number of hidden layer neurons (N) (Nabipour, Qasem, Salwana, & Baghban, 2020). SVM is a supervised machine-learning technique for classification and regression tasks. In this approach, the hyperplane that best separates the categories of data is identified by maximising the interval between the hyperplane and the nearest data point. Additionally, kernel functions can be used for nonlinear data and feature selection (Aburomman & Reaz, 2017). The LSSVM is an improved version of the SVM that maps the input vector to a high-dimensional feature space in which the optimal decision function. To construct the optimal decision function, the structural risk is minimised, and the kernel function in the original space is employed instead of the dot product operation in the high-dimensional feature space. The use of least squares estimation to determine the model parameters yields computational savings. For large data sets, the LSSVM is faster than the SVM. Both the SVM and LSSVM are effective for classification and regression tasks, but the LSSVM may be more suitable for noisy or large data sets (Nabipour et al., 2020).

The phase of the CQDs solutions prepared in this study was characterised using transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 A.B, the particle size was distributed within 2.0–5.0 nm with an average of 3.30 nm, indicating that the CQDs had favourable dispersion. Fig. 1C presents the energy spectrum of the CQDs and reveals the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulphur in the sample. Fig. 1D shows the FTIR spectrum of the CQDs, the absorption peaks at 833.94 cm−1 are attributed to C–C stretching vibration, 1134.79 cm−1 to C–O stretching vibration, 1400.68 cm−1 to C–OH in-plane bending vibration, 1576.78 cm−1 to COO- anti asymmetric stretching vibration, 1629.74 cm−1 to CO stretching vibration, 2076.09 cm−1 to NCS anti asymmetric stretching vibration, 2924.47 cm−1 to CH vibration, and the absorption peak at 3439.66 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration. (Chen et al., 2019). In addition, the optical properties of the CQDs solutions were investigated. The solutions were found to be brownish yellow under natural light irradiation and bright blue under 365-nm UV irradiation. To better describe their colour, we determined the corresponding CIE colour coordinates: (0.148, 0.104), as shown in Fig. 1E. To further investigate the dots’ optical properties, their UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained (Fig. 1F); the absorption peak in the spectra appears at 340 nm wavelength, which may be attributed to the n → π ⁎ leap of the CO bond. (Dong et al., 2013). As revealed by the fluorescence spectrogram, the maximum excitation wavelength of the CQDs was 340 nm, and the strongest emission occurred at the wavelength of 440 nm.

Stability is an important index for evaluating the quality of CQDs. Three sets of parallel experiments were conducted to detect the intensity of the fluorescence of the CQDs solution every 5 min; the change in the dots’ fluorescence intensity over time is illustrated in Fig. 1G with RSD (n = 19) = 1.08%. The results showed that the CQDs had excellent photostability in aqueous solution, with super-stable fluorescence intensity after continuous excitation over a period of 90 min. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots under different storage conditions was examined every 5 days to investigate the stability of carbon quantum dots, as shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots stored at −4 °C under light-avoidance conditions remained basically unchanged in one month, with an RSD = 0.25%. The fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots at room temperature under light-avoidance and at room temperature under light-absorbance conditions decreased by 19% and 76%, respectively, indicating that the temperature and light have a greater effect on them. In addition, the effect of different pH solutions on the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots was also explored, as shown in Fig. S1B. The fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots did not change much between pH = 4–11, but the fluorescence intensity was weakened accordingly when it was in the case of strong acid (pH = 2–3) or strong base (pH = 12–13).

The effect of various metal ions on the fluorescence detection intensity under a given reaction context was investigated. The relationships of EGCG concentration with the quenching of the CQDs’ fluorescence intensities after the addition of 0.1 mL of 10−3 mol/L Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Co2+ and Cu2+ are shown in Fig. S2, respectively. When Fe3+ was added (Fig. S2B), the fluorescence intensity was not linearly related to the concentration over the concentration range 0.01–10 μg/mL, and R2 was 0.9765 over the concentration range 10–30 μg/mL, indicating that Fe3+ was not the best choice for the additive to CQDs for detecting low concentrations of EGCG. When Fe2+ was added (Fig. S2C), the fluorescence intensity was again not linearly related to the concentration in the concentration range 0.01–20 μg/mL, and R2 was 0.9796 in the concentration range 20–50 μg/mL; we thus concluded that Fe2+ was also not the ideal medium. The addition of Zn2+ did not result in a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration in the concentration range 0.01–1 μg/mL, and the R2 was 0.9397 in the concentration range 1–50 μg/mL (Fig. S2D). When Co2+ was added, the CQDs trapped EGCG through functional groups on their surface, whereas the Co2+ formed a red-brown inner complex salt precipitate with phenolics; the addition of Co2+ caused CQDs agglomeration, leading to fluorescence quenching. The CQDs responded well to various concentrations of EGCG, leading to a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9818 (Fig. S2E). Fig. S2F shows the overall trend in the fluorescence intensity for various EGCG concentrations when Cu2+ was added; this intensity decreased with increasing EGCG concentration, with the corresponding correlation coefficient being R2 = 0.8453, which was slightly lower that achieved using Co2+. Co-CQDs are thus a superior sensing system that has potential for use in monitoring the quality-related composition of black tea.

The effect of the quantitative relationship between Co2+ and EGCG on the quenching of the CQDs fluorescent probe was further investigated (Fig. 2 ). In the system containing 0.1 mL of Co2+ and 0.05 mL of EGCG (Fig. 2A), the detection system was found to discriminate poorly between low concentrations. In the system containing 0.1 mL of Co2+ and 0.1 mL of EGCG (Fig. 2B), the spectral curves corresponding to different concentrations were well differentiated, with the coefficient of correlation between the fluorescence quenching rate and concentration being R2 = 0.9818. In the system containing 0.1 mL of Co2+ and 0.2 mL of EGCG (Fig. 2C), the fluorescence intensity decreased very slowly at EGCG concentrations >30 μg/mL, probably due to the impending saturation of the binding between Co2+ and EGCG. Therefore, the optimal combination was identified as 2.7 mL of pure water +0.1 mL of CQDs solution +0.1 mL of Co + 0.1 mL of sample solution and was used in the next experiments. The results of these studies will be important for the trace detection of substances contained in tea leaves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of excitation wavelength and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of the CQDs and the detection time of the CQDs with samples were investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy. CQDs are suited to analyte detection because their fluorescence is excitation dependent in most cases (Zu et al., 2017). As can be seen from (Fig. 3 A), the fluorescence emission of the CQDs exhibited strong dependence on the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength was varied in the range 300–400 nm (at 10 nm intervals), the fluorescence intensity increased and then gradually decreased with excitation wavelength. The intensity was highest for an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelength was 440 nm, which corroborated the results of the CQDs characterisation. After that, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dots was promptly characterised by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The relative quantum yield of the carbon quantum dots was calculated according to the following equation (Cao et al., 2022).
(
Q Y
)
S
=
(
Q Y
)
s t
(
K s
/
K
s t
)
(
ŋ s
/
ŋ
s t
)
Where (QY)s and (QY)st denote the relative quantum yields of the carbon quantum dots and quinine sulfate, respectively, Ks and Kst denote the slopes of the carbon quantum dots and quinine sulfate, respectively, and η
s and η
st denote the refractive indices of the solvents of the carbon quantum dots and quinine sulfate, respectively. The refractive index of sulfuric acid is 1.33 and that of water is 1.33. The fluorescence yield of carbon quantum dots prepared in this study (Table S3) was calculated to be 81%.

In order to verify the feasibility of applying the CQDs/Co2+ fluorescent probe to determine the degree of fermentation of black tea, the effects of other components in tea infusion on the quenching of the carbon quantum dot fluorescence were investigated, and as shown in Fig. S3. CaCl2、KCl、MgCl2、NaCl、l-Theanine、Theophylline、l-Glutamic acid、Boc-d-Glutamine、l(+)-Arginine、dl-Serine、l-Aspartic acid、Sucrose had no significant effect on the quenching of the carbon quantum dot fluorescent probe. The response time of the Co-CQDs detection system for the detection of samples was investigated by dividing the fluorescence intensity detected at each time point by the fluorescence intensity detected at the first time, as shown in Fig. 3B. The carbon quantum dot detection system can respond in real time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of CQDs’ fluorescence by representative components of black tea and development of standard curves
ce:section.ce:para: Fermentation is a key process in the formation of black tea's flavour substances and functional components such as TFs, theobromine, and other polyphenolic polymers (Wu et al., 2019). To monitor the changes in the content of these components of black tea, 12 representative components of black tea—C, EC, GC, EGC, EGCG, ECG, GCG, GA, TF, TF-3-G, TF-3′-G, and TF-D-G—were investigated. The fluorescence behaviour of the Co-CQDs system for the 12 representative components was investigated separately. A standard curve and an equation expressing the coefficient of correlation between concentration and fluorescence intensity (F0/F − 1) were established using the mean values of the fluorescence spectra obtained after pretreatment; the results are shown in Fig. 4 A–L, and R2 > 97%. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after addition of the samples, respectively, for emission at a wavelength of 440 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the reaction system decreased with concentration within the range 0.01–50 μg/mL, and the rate of fluorescence quenching exhibited a good linear relationship with concentration. This was because the analyte and Co-CQDs bound and clustered together, which quenched the carbon quantum dots' fluorescence. Because the fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated with the concentration of the quencher over a certain range, the Co-CQDs can be considered a highly promising detection probe in the field of tea analysis (Li et al., 2019; Chen, Gooding, & Liu, 2019).

In addition, the LOD was calculated for each standard by LOD = 3 N/S (N = 0.00005626). Based on the equation, the detection limits for all 12 key substances were less than 0.046 μg/mL. The slope of their fitted lines indicated that standards differed in their different fluorescence quenching abilities, which may have been due to the different number of functional groups that could bind to the CQDs in the reaction system for different substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative differentiation of black tea samples with different degrees of fermentation
ce:section.ce:para: PCA and HCA were performed using spectral data from seven black tea samples with various fermentation times. As shown in Fig. 5 AB, the PCA resulted in possible sample groupings of fermentation levels. Principal components 1 and 2 contributed 86.3% and 10.0% of the variance in the spectral data, respectively, indicating that these components could explain 96.3% of the tea sample information. The points representing insufficiently fermented, moderately fermented, and excessively fermented samples were favourably clustered, with clear separation between clusters. Conversely, HCA objectively provided fermentation clusters that were most in agreement with the results of the sensory review. The insufficiently fermented, moderately fermented, and excessively fermented groups comprised samples with fermentation lasting 0–2, 3–5 h, and ≥6 h, respectively.

This study also employed supervised algorithms to construct three discriminatory qualitative models and compared the performance of these models (Table 1 ). The results revealed that the discrimination rates for the ELM calibration and prediction sets were 77.14% and 75.71%, respectively, which was due to the disadvantages of the ELM model in that the learning rate is difficult to determine and the model tends to fall into local minima. Both the SVM and LSSVM achieved better results, with the LSSVM obtaining the optimal discrimination results (regularisation parameter γ = 13.1502 and RBF function parameter σ2 = 42.8841) because it was more robust to noise and outliers in the data. The LSSVM model had a correct classification rate of 100% in fermentation level on both the training and prediction sets.

In this study, standard curves for catechins, TFs, and gallic acid were established, and a model for determining the fermentation quality of black tea was developed. The results demonstrate the feasibility of a fluorescence-quenching reaction based on CQDs probes and target compounds for monitoring the fermentation quality of black tea. Importantly, the method proposed in this study is not susceptible to environmental influences, has simple sample pre-processing, requires no data pre-processing, is easy to operate, provides fast and accurate results, and is inexpensive. Although traditional methods of chemical analysis methods such as HPLC can produce objective and accurate results on the basis of the key components they detect, they are technically demanding for the operator in addition to being time-consuming, which makes them unsuited to most practical applications. Many studies have obtained favourable quality monitoring results by using emerging nondestructive testing methods such as computer vision system (CVS). Ren et al. (Ren et al., 2021) used a charge-coupled device camera to obtain images of leaves and judged the quality of black tea by analysing the RGB values of the images; the results revealed that the best LSSVM model outperformed other models with a discrimination rate of 100% on both the training and prediction sets. However, during the fermentation of black tea, polyphenols undergo strong oxidation and reduction reactions, leading to changes in the colour and intrinsic composition of the fermented leaves, and CVS is adversely affected by differences in leaf colour between varieties. Near-infrared spectroscopy and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy have been employed to monitor the quality of black tea fermentation. Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2021) predicted indicators of the quality of black tea fermentation by performing near-infrared spectroscopy, and the results showed that the relative percentage difference (RPD) of catechins, TFs/TP, and TP/FAA content were 2.621, 2.544, and 3.472, respectively. By constructing a 1D-ResNet18 prediction model, Luo et al. (Luo et al., 2022) employed surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy data to successfully differentiate fermentation stages; the model's accuracy was 83.33%. Quantitative models were developed for C, EGCG, EC, ECG, CC, TP, TB, TR, and TFs, with the corresponding predicted R2 values being 0.81, 0.82, 0.73, 0.79, 0.58, 0.5, 0.8, 0.67, and 0.67, respectively. However, the large dimensionality of the data, cumbersome processing, and high cost of equipment hinder their applicability to the online monitoring of black tea fermentation quality. By contrast, quantum dot probes have the advantages of being inexpensive, being made from abundantly available synthetic materials (Purbia & Paria, 2016), being amenable to simple methods of synthesis (Roy, Preeyanka, Majhi, Seth, & Sarkar, 2018), and offering fast detection and intuitive results (Thulasi, Kathiravan, & Jhonsi, 2020). Selvaraju et al. (Selvaraju et al., 2022) successfully synthesised luminescent CQDs for the rapid detection of heavy metal ions in industrial wastewater; they achieved an LOD of 1.2 μM and high selectivity and sensitivity. In the present study, the substances that changed during the fermentation of black tea were organic and contained relatively complex functional groups. No other studies have explored the use of CQDs for monitoring the fermentation quality of black tea; however, CQDs fluorescent probes appear to be promising in scenarios requiring rapid detection of such quality. Of the common fluorescent probes, metallic fluorescent particles, usually made from precious metals, are expensive (Shu, Wang, Su, & Zhang, 2018), and the application of organic fluorescent polymers is limited by their toxicity (Li et al., 2020). Compared with other inorganic nanodots, CQDs have the advantages of being producible from a wide range of raw materials and having simple synthesis, stable properties, and diverse response modes (Xu, Lu, Li, & Yang, 2018).

Although this study yielded satisfactory results in identifying the fermentation quality of black tea, there are also limitations. The single-emission carbon quantum dots prepared in this study have a fixed emission wavelength and a single data type, which limits their flexibility in multi-channel detection. Despite these limitations, single-emission carbon quantum dots remain an active area of research. Compared to single-emission fluorescent probes, ratiometric fluorescent probes can detect the concentration of a substance to be measured by using the change in the ratio of the fluorescence intensities of the two fluorescent peaks, which reduces the effects caused by various error factors, improves the signal-to-noise ratio, and thus increases the sensitivity (Han et al., 2022). Therefore, future research will focus on the development of ratiometric fluorescent probes to improve sensitivity in complex backgrounds.

Sodium citrate as the carbon source resulted in the preparation of carbon quantum dots with satisfactory fluorescence yield (81%), and the established assay was selective for tea polyphenols, and the stable carbon quantum dots are expected to provide reliable performance for the detection of the whole process of black tea fermentation. In addition, the standard curves of 12 key substances in the black tea fermentation process were established, with good linearity in the concentration range of 0.01–50 μg/mL, R2 ≥ 97%, and LOD between 0.011 and 0.046 μg/mL. What's more, combined with the chemometrics method, the LSSVM model was used to achieve 100% correct discrimination of black tea samples with different fermentation degrees. Overall, this study presents a new technique for rapid detection of black tea fermentation quality, the whole process takes about 10 min, which combines carbon quantum dot nanotechnology and fluorescence spectroscopy to provide a new solution for the intelligent processing of black tea.

Participants gave informed consent via the statement "I am aware that my responses are confidential, and I agree to participate in this sensory evaluation" where an affirmative reply was required to enter the sensory evaluation. They were able to withdraw from the sensory evaluation at any time without giving a reason. The tea products evaluated were safe for consumption.

Shuci Cao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuai Dong: Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yurong Chen: Supervision, Software, Data curation. Jingfei Shen: Visualization, Supervision, Investigation. Qingqing Cui: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Changying Sun: Supervision, Resources, Investigation. Jingming Ning: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis. Luqing Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Nature Science Foundation of China (32202543), National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD1601102), National Key Research and Development Program (2023YFD1601300), Key Research and Development Program of Anhui Province (202104h04020023), the earmarked fund for CARS (CARS-19).
